context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 5


Problem 5.1 (20 pts)

In a lab, a graduate student was studying a string of length L, fixed at both ends.

(a) Find the total energy of a vibration of the string oscillating with its nth normal mode with amplitude A. The tension in the string is T and its total mass is M.
    Hint: Consider the integrated kinetic energy at the instant when the string is straight, such that the potential energy due to the vibration is zero.
(b) Calculate the total energy of vibration of the same string if it is vibrating in the following superposition of normal modes. Assume that it is the sum of the energies of the two modes taken separately.
    ψ(x,t) = A1·sin(πx/L)·cos(ω1·t) + A3·sin(3πx/L)·cos(ω3·t − π/4)


Problem 5.2 (20 pts)

A string of length L, tension T, and mass per unit length ρL fixed at both ends is distorted as shown in Figure 1. The height of the square pulse is h. The string is constrained to vibrate only in the vertical direction. At t = 0, the string is carefully released such that the initial velocity of the string is 0.

[Figure 1: A string fixed at x = 0 and x = L, with a square pulse of height h and width L/4 centered at x = L/2.]

(a) What is the speed of a progressing wave on this string?
(b) Plot to scale the shape of the string at t = (L/4)·√(ρL/T).
(c) Plot to scale the shape of the string at t = L·√(ρL/T).


Problem 5.3 (20 pts)

Consider a string of mass density ρ under tension T. A pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = y0·exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]
Energy in the wave is a sum of the kinetic energy of the moving string and the potential energy of the string deformation, integrated over the whole length of the string.

(a) For the displacement given above, find an expression for the kinetic energy density of the string at some time t and sketch the result as a function of x.
(b) Find an expression for the potential energy density of the string. Compare it with the kinetic energy density.
(c) Find an expression for the total energy of the pulse in terms of T, y0 and σ. Check the units. Recall that the energy should vary as the square of the amplitude.
    Hint: You may need to use the Gaussian integral ∫_{−∞}^{∞} x^2·e^(−a·x^2) dx = (1/2a)·√(π/a).
(d) Find an expression for the power (Watts) flowing across the point x = x0 as a function of time. Sketch the result.


Problem 5.4 (20 pts)

Consider a string with linear density ρL split into two pieces. The two halves are attached to a massless ring which slides vertically without friction on a rod at x = 0 as shown in Figure 2. One of the two halves is stretched in the negative x direction with tension TL, the other is stretched in the positive x direction with tension TR. Note that the vertical rod is necessary to balance the horizontal forces on the massless string arising from the two strings with different tensions.
Assume that there is a traveling wave of amplitude A coming from the negative x direction.

[Figure 2: Two string halves with a massless ring at x = 0 — the left half under tension TL, the right half under tension TR, joined at a ring that slides on a vertical rod.]

(a) Assuming the incident wave has the functional form ψ(x,t) = α·cos(kx − ωt), write the general expression for the string displacement ψ(x,t) on both sides of the ring.
(b) Write the expressions for the boundary conditions at x = 0. (Hint: since the ring is massless, the total force on the ring is 0.)
(c) Find the reflection (R) and transmission (T) coefficients for this system.
(d) Show that the energy is conserved at the junction.
(e) What are the reflection and transmission coefficients in the following three cases: TR = TL, TR ≫ TL, and TR ≪ TL? Does your result make sense?


Problem 5.5 (20 pts)

A string of mass density ρL under tension T is attached by a massless ring to a wire covered with a viscous grease. The ring experiences a vertical drag force Fy = −b·(∂y/∂t) when the end of the string moves.

[Figure: the end of the string attached to a ring on a wire covered with grease, providing a viscous drag force.]

(a) Use Newton's Law applied to the ring to find the boundary condition at the end of the string as a relation between the partial derivatives of y(x,t) at that point. (Hint: there are three forces acting on the ring: string tension, normal force from the wire, and the vertical drag force.)
(b) Show that the boundary condition is satisfied by an incident pulse f(t − x/v) and reflected pulse g(t + x/v). Find g in terms of f.
(c) Show that your result has the proper behavior in the limits b → 0 (free slip) and b → ∞ (clamped string).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m doing Problem Set 5 for vibrations and waves, and honestly I’m pretty lost already. Could we do 5.1 first? I don’t really see how to get the total energy for the nth normal mode.
exchanges.tutor:
Good starting point! Let's build from the basics.

The hint says to look at the instant when the string is straight — meaning all the energy is kinetic at that moment. So, what does the displacement of the string look like for the nth normal mode, and what would you write for its velocity at that instant?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting Problem 5.1(a) and feeling lost. I should use guided discovery to help them engage with the physics rather than giving them a formula. The hint in the problem already points toward evaluating kinetic energy when the string is straight (potential energy = 0), so I should ask them to think about what the string looks like at that moment and what expression they would write for kinetic energy. I'll keep it bite-sized and Socratic, starting with the most fundamental question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: I think the displacement is something like \(y(x,t)=A\sin(n\pi x/L)\cos(\omega t)\), but then I get confused about what “straight” means here. Does that mean pick the time when \(\cos(\omega t)=0\), and then I just differentiate with respect to \(t\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both counts — good reasoning! So go ahead and write out the velocity ∂y/∂t, then evaluate it at the moment when cos(ωt) = 0. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the normal mode displacement and correctly reasoned that 'straight' means cos(ωt)=0, which is when the string passes through equilibrium. They also correctly identified that they need to differentiate with respect to t to get velocity. This is good progress. I should affirm this and push them to actually write out the velocity expression at that moment, which is the next concrete step.

exchanges.student: I get \(\partial y/\partial t=-A\omega \sin(n\pi x/L)\sin(\omega t)\), so at the straight-string instant it seems like the magnitude is \(A\omega \sin(n\pi x/L)\). I’m not sure what to do next though — do I use \(dm=(M/L)\,dx\) and integrate \(\tfrac12 v^2\,dm\) over the whole string?
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's exactly the right setup. Go ahead and write out the full integral and evaluate it. What do you get when you integrate sin²(nπx/L) over 0 to L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the velocity at the right instant and correctly identified the approach for kinetic energy: using dm = (M/L)dx and integrating (1/2)v² dm. This is exactly right. I should affirm this and prompt them to set up and evaluate the integral, which involves a standard sin² integral they should be able to handle.

exchanges.student: I think that integral is \(L/2\), but I’m already losing track of where the \(n\) is supposed to show up. If it just gives \(E=\frac14 M A^2\omega^2\), then how do I turn that into something with \(T\), \(L\), and \(n\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good, the integral result is correct! Now you just need one more piece: what is ω_n for the nth normal mode of a string fixed at both ends? You should have seen this earlier in the course — it relates to the wave speed and the boundary conditions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly evaluated the integral and got E = (1/4)MA²ω². Now they're asking how to bring in T, L, and n. This is a good question — they need to use the dispersion relation for a string fixed at both ends to express ω in terms of T, M, L, and n. I should prompt them to recall what ω_n is for the nth normal mode of a fixed-fixed string, rather than giving it to them.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember \(k_n=n\pi/L\) and maybe \(v=\sqrt{T/\mu}\), but I keep mixing up \(M/L\), \(\mu\), and \(\rho_L\). Could you just lay out the whole chain cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
You have all the right pieces! Just to clear up the notation: μ, ρ_L, and M/L are all the same thing — linear mass density.

Now, given k_n = nπ/L and v = ω/k, can you write ω_n? Then substitute into your energy expression.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to lay out the whole chain for them, which would essentially give them the solution. I should not do that. However, they do have the right pieces — they know k_n and v. I should affirm what they have and ask them to connect the pieces themselves, one step at a time. I'll prompt them to write ω_n from k_n and v, and clarify that μ and ρ_L are the same thing (mass per unit length), which should resolve their confusion without giving away the answer.

exchanges.student: I think \(\omega_n = (n\pi/L)\sqrt{T/\mu}\), so then \(E=\frac14 M A^2 \omega_n^2\), but I’m not confident simplifying it. I’m also not sure if part (b) means I should add the two energies even though the modes are happening at the same time.
exchanges.tutor:
You're on the right track with both. For part (a): try substituting M = μL into your expression and see what simplifies.

For part (b): the problem explicitly tells you to assume the total energy is the sum of the two separate mode energies. Do you have a sense of *why* that might be physically valid for normal modes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived ω_n and the energy expression. They can simplify by substituting M = μL into E = (1/4)MA²ω_n². I should nudge them to do that substitution themselves rather than doing it for them. On part (b), their intuition to question whether energies simply add is good physical thinking — I should ask them why the problem statement says to assume the energies add separately, prompting them to think about orthogonality of modes, without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: Not really — I’m kind of fuzzy on the whole “modes don’t mix” idea. Could you explain part (b) more broadly, like from the physical picture and the math, because I feel like I’m missing the big idea not just the algebra?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to want the bigger picture. Here's a question to guide you: when you compute the total kinetic energy for the superposition, you'll get cross terms involving products of the two modes. What do you get when you integrate sin(nπx/L)·sin(mπx/L) over 0 to L, when n ≠ m?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of why normal modes don't mix energetically. This is an important concept, but I shouldn't lecture them through it. Instead, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about what happens when you integrate a product of two different sine functions over the string length — this is the mathematical heart of orthogonality, and it connects directly to why the cross terms vanish in the energy. I'll keep it brief and pointed.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember that orthogonality result well enough to trust myself, and I’m getting overwhelmed by all the pieces. Can you just show me the full setup for part (b), including where the cross terms go?
exchanges.tutor: I won't show you the full setup, but let's make it concrete and small. Can you just try evaluating this one integral: ∫₀ᴸ sin(πx/L)·sin(3πx/L) dx? Use a trig product-to-sum identity. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show the full setup, which I cannot do. However, they are expressing frustration and feeling overwhelmed. I should be supportive but firm, and redirect them to the specific integral they need to evaluate rather than showing the full setup. I'll keep it very focused — just ask them to try the specific integral sin(πx/L)·sin(3πx/L) over 0 to L, which is concrete and manageable. This is the key step they need, and working it out themselves will give them confidence.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the identity too, sorry. I feel like I need a quick refresher on why different sine modes integrate to zero before I can do this.
exchanges.tutor:
Here's the identity: sin(A)·sin(B) = ½[cos(A−B) − cos(A+B)]. That's all you need.

Apply it to your integral and you should see quickly why it vanishes. But keep in mind — once you confirm the cross terms are zero, part (b) is essentially just applying your part (a) result twice. Don't lose sight of that bigger picture!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the trig identity and feeling overwhelmed. They've now been circling around the orthogonality concept for several exchanges. Per the rules, when a student is frustrated or going in circles, I can be less Socratic and use a relevant example, but still not give the answer. I'll give them the specific identity they need as a brief reminder, then ask them to apply it to the integral themselves. I should also gently remind them of the bigger picture so they don't get too bogged down here.

exchanges.student: Okay, so I’d get cos\((2\pi x/L)\) and cos\((4\pi x/L)\), and over \(0\) to \(L\) those probably average to zero, I think. I’m still not fully sure how to turn that into the final energy expression for the superposition though.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — those integrals are zero. So the cross terms vanish, which means the total energy really is just the sum of the two separate mode energies.

You already have the energy formula for a single mode from part (a). Can you now write out E_total for the superposition in part (b) by applying that formula to each mode individually?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out that the cross terms vanish. Now they need to connect this back to part (b) and write the total energy as a sum of the two individual mode energies using their result from part (a). This is a straightforward application of what they've already derived. We've spent several exchanges on orthogonality — it's time to push them to write the final expression themselves. I should affirm their result and ask them to write out the final answer using their part (a) formula applied to each mode.
